title: Carved hawthorn walking stick
subtitle: A hawthorn stick with a carved handle, cut from a coppice or hedgerow
excerpt: A walking stick cut from a naturally straight hawthorn stem, seasoned for twelve months, then carved at the handle end and finished with oil and a rubber ferrule. Hawthorn is the traditional English walking-stick wood: naturally hard, knobbly, and durable. The carving is limited to the handle; the shaft is left with natural bark or stripped and polished.
sourceNotes: Victorian walking-stick and country-craft manuals for stick dressing and handle carving; Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861) for hawthorn and hedgerow wood use.

glossaryTerms.definition: A native British hedgerow shrub (Crataegus monogyna) producing dense, very hard wood with a distinctive knobbly grain. Cut in winter when dormant; must be seasoned slowly to avoid checking.

Controlled drying of freshly cut wood to reduce moisture content before carving or use. Green hawthorn cut in winter should be dried slowly in a cool, dry place for at least twelve months before working.
A metal or rubber cap fitted to the tip of a walking stick to protect the end-grain from splitting on hard ground. Rubber ferrules slip onto the end; metal ferrules are hammered on and often pinned.

content.text: Keep both hands behind the cutting edge at all times. Carve away from the body. Secure the stick in a vice or on a shaving horse before drawknife work; do not hold it free in the hand against the pull of the drawknife.
walking stick is cut in winter and then left:

takes twelve months and cannot be rushed. The best sticks have a gentle natural curve at the handle and a section of knobbly side-shoots that have been removed flush; these produce the characteristically dotted surface of a traditional hawthorn shaft.

Cut hawthorn in December or January when the sap is fully down. Select a stem 20 to 25 mm in diameter at the base and at least 1.2 m long. Cut at an angle close to the root for a clean separation. Remove side shoots flush with the main shaft using secateurs or the sloyd knife. Seal the cut ends with wax or dilute glue to slow moisture loss from the end-grain and reduce the risk of checking.

Hang the sticks upright in a dry, ventilated space for twelve months. Do not dry them in direct heat; rapid moisture loss causes splits along the grain. At the end of twelve months the stick should feel light and ring clearly when tapped.

On a seasoned stick, strip the bark from the lower shaft using a drawknife, working with the grain in long smooth passes. The knot positions from removed side shoots make natural grip points and should be preserved, not removed. Work the lower shaft to a smooth surface with a spokeshave, leaving the natural taper of the stem.

The handle is the natural curve at the top of the stick where a root or branch join gives a usable angle. Shape the outer curve with a sloyd knife, removing the bark and refining the form to fit comfortably in the hand. A simple thumb groove is sufficient: a 12 mm wide, 4 mm deep channel carved into the inner face of the curve where the thumb rests. For a decorative handle, a medium-sweep gouge can add simple relief work to the outer face.

Apply two to three coats of linseed or tung oil to the entire stick, rubbing each coat well in and wiping off the excess. The oil will darken the wood and bring out the natural variation in grain colour. Fit a rubber
to the bottom tip: cut the tip square with the bow saw, sand the end-grain smooth, and press the ferrule on.

items.symptom: The stick develops a long split along the shaft during seasoning
items.cause: Moisture loss too rapid, usually from drying near a heat source
items.fix: A split that develops in the first weeks of drying is usually minor and will stabilise as the wood dries out. Sand the split edges smooth and seal with thin glue. A major split that runs more than 15 cm along the shaft means the stick is weakened; use it as a carving practice piece rather than a walking stick.
